tree.heading: Introduction

tree.heading: Exercise
tree.content_preview:
A number of systematic reviews and meta -analyses have considered whether exercise reduces BP in dialysis. Most studies have focused on intra -dialytic exercise.

tree.heading: Background
tree.content_preview: High blood pressure (BP) is very common in people with end stage kidney disease (ESKD) on dialysis. It is a risk factor for heart disease, which is the most common cause of death in people on dialysis

tree.heading: How did we develop these guidelines?
tree.content_preview: These guidelines were developed by a broad group of experienced kidney care professionals. They went through existing research evidence to give guidance on how best to manage BP in people on dialysis.

tree.heading: The topic areas were:
tree.content_preview:
How to measure BP
What BP we should be aiming for
Lifestyle measures and medications to help with BP control
Modifications to dialysis and dialysate fluid
Assessment of dry weight
Considerations that

tree.heading: How should we measure blood pressure and what blood pressure should we be aiming for in adults on dialysis?
tree.content_preview: We found limited good quality information to tell us which BP measurement should be used in people on dialysis. Most research studies in haemodialysis patients have used 'usual' pre- and post-dialysis

tree.heading: These are:
tree.content_preview:
Aim for a pre -dialysis systolic (upper) reading of between 140 and 165 mmHg.
For patients whose BP is stable during dialysis try to aim for the lower end of this range.
For those with few or no other

tree.heading: What lifestyle measures help control BP?
tree.content_preview: We recommend that dialysis patients restrict their salt intake to no more than 5 grams (less than a level teaspoon full) per day. Renal dieticians can assist patients in how to calculate the salt cont

tree.heading: How may modifications of dialysis and dialysate fluid help?
tree.content_preview: Changing the type and duration of dialysis treatment or changing the composition of dialysis fluid may help to improve BP control. We suggest that those with high BP, increasing the duration of haemod

tree.heading: How should dry weight be determined?
tree.content_preview: Dialysis patients who have too much (overloaded) or too little fluid (dehydrated) in their circulation have worse outcomes. So it is important for dialysis staff to correctly assess each patient's 'fl

tree.heading: What medications help with blood pressure on dialysis?
tree.content_preview: Many people having dialysis will require BP lowering medications. The pros and cons of the various types of medications are beyond the scope of this summary. In brief, we suggest beta -blockers (e.g.

tree.heading: What is particularly important in Children & Young People (CYP)?
tree.content_preview: High BP is common in CYP on dialysis, and this can lead to long term changes to the heart. Unlike in the adult population, we do not have clear information from research that high BP directly leads to

tree.heading: Appendix B: PICOS for literature search, search strategies & PRISMA flowchart
Eligibility Criteria:
tree.content_preview: P opulation: Adults, children and young people receiving dialysis (haemodialysis, haemodiafiltration and peritoneal dialysis) for end stage renal disease

tree.heading: I ntervention:
tree.content_preview: Any intervention intended to reduce blood pressure (BP), to include but not limited to: blood pressure lowering medication; target/dry weight reduction or optimization; dialysis prescription, includin

tree.heading: Exclusion Criteria:
tree.content_preview:
The search strategy will eliminate studies solely focusing on:
Acute haemodialysis
Acute peritoneal dialysis
Continuous veno -venous haemofiltration (CVVHF) and other acute renal replacement modalitie
